elements.sent-2411-001: We study the structure, interstellar absorption, color-magnitude diagrams, kinematics, and dynamical state of embedded star clusters in the star-forming region associated with the giant molecular cloud G174+2.5.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our investigation is based on photometric data from the UKIDSS Galactic Plane Survey catalog and astrometric data from the Gaia DR3 catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-003: First, we recover all the known embedded clusters and candidate clusters in the region using surface density maps.
elements.sent-2411-004: Then, for the detected clusters, we determine their general parameters: the center positions, radii, number of stars, and reddening.
elements.sent-2411-005: To evaluate the reddening, we use both the NICEST algorithm and the Q-method.
elements.sent-2411-006: Both methods produce consistent extinction maps in the regions of the four studied clusters.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, the Q-method yields a much smaller color scatter in the CMD.
elements.sent-2411-008: For four clusters in particular (S235~North-West, S235~A-B-C, S235~Central, and S235~East1+East2), we were able to compute individual membership probabilities, the cluster distances, the cluster masses, and their average proper motions.
elements.sent-2411-009: By building on these results, we have studied the clusters' kinematics and dynamics .
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, we estimate the mass of the gas component and the star formation efficiency (SFE) in the regions of these four clusters.
elements.sent-2411-011: Finally, we provide an estimate of the total energy of the stellar and gas components in the area of these four clusters to determine whether the clusters are bound (here we consider a `cluster' as the system `stars + gas').
elements.sent-2411-012: The gravitational bound strongly depends on the region for which we estimate the gas mass.
elements.sent-2411-013: If we consider the mass of the entire cloud, all these four clusters turn out to be bound.
elements.sent-2411-014: Young star clusters are among the main sources of information about the process, rate, and efficiency of star formation.
elements.sent-2411-015: Their study allows one to establish the form of the initial mass function, to probe the position of the spiral arms of the Galaxy and to investigate the gravitational interaction of newly born stars and their interaction with the parental gas cloud on a variety of scales.
elements.sent-2411-016: In general, they are excellent benchmarks of the theory of star formation \citep{Lada03,GCa2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The employment of thermal imaging cameras and spectrometers with optical and infrared-optimized telescopes over the past decades \citep[for example,][]{Skrutskie06, Lawrence07, Werner04 had made it possible to study systematically the clusters' emergence inside molecular clouds.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Clusters in this early evolutionary stage are often referred to as embedded star clusters \citep{Stahler18 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Much work has been done over the years to unravel the properties of these objects \citep{Lada03, Sobolev17, Stahler18 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Perhaps, the most important outcome is that most stars form in such proto-clusters, and that only a small fraction of these proto-clusters survive their emergence from molecular clouds and become long-lived open clusters.
elements.sent-2411-021: Currently, about 3000 embedded clusters and cluster candidates are known, and the corresponding information can be found in various studies and catalogs \citep[see, for example,][etc.]{Bica03, Lada03, Kumar06, Solin12, Camargo16, Ryu18 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In particular, the studies of \citet{Arzoumanian11 and \citet{Andre14 have shown that stars form in thin filaments of molecular gas.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: At the same time, observations indicate that dense gas in the star-forming molecular clouds \citep{Schisano14, Konyves15 has a multi-scale, fractal structure.}
elements.sent-2411-024: At close distances, we can clearly distinguish the fine structure of clouds~-- thin filaments with stars forming in them.
elements.sent-2411-025: For more distant regions, on the contrary, small structures are not resolved well during observations, but large ones are visible.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this case, we cannot trace in which particular thin filaments individual stars are born, but it becomes clear that concentrations of young stars are located within large-scale structures.
elements.sent-2411-027: The origin of filaments and their general properties are still not completely understood, but filaments have become one of the main components of the modern star formation paradigm \citep{Andre14 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: For the purpose of this study, it is very important to underline that star clusters are found to form in the large-scale gaseous filaments.
elements.sent-2411-029: This study is devoted to the search and characterization of young star clusters in one of the nearest star formation regions, which is associated with the giant molecular cloud (GMC) G174+2.5.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this region, \citet{Bieging16 indicate evidence for gas filaments of different scales.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Besides, this area contains four regions of ionized hydrogen from the Sharpless catalog visible in the optical range: Sh2-231, Sh2-232, Sh2-233, and Sh2-235.
elements.sent-2411-032: The G174+2.5 complex is located in the Perseus spiral arm, and the estimate of its distance ranges from 1.4 to 2.5 kpc \citep{Georgelin73, Israel78, Evans81, Foster15 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The most valuable estimate of this complex distance by the trigonometric parallaxes of the maser sources was 1.56 kpc \citep{Burns15 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The HII region Sh2-235 is generated by the O9.5V star BD$+35^{\circ 1201$ \citep{Georgelin73 , which ionizes and scatters the surrounding molecular gas.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The Sh2-235 molecular complex is a region of ongoing star formation, where one can observe objects in different evolutionary phases.
elements.sent-2411-036: There are also young star clusters around Sh2-235.
elements.sent-2411-037: These clusters were detected from the analysis of Spitzer data in the vicinity of Sh2-235 \citep{Allen05, Chavarria14 and from 2MASS images in the direction of the IRAS source 05382+3547 in the same vicinity \citep{Kumar06 .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Recently, the gas component of the region, young stellar objects and their spatial distribution have been studied in more details \citep[see, for example,][]{Porras+2000, Dewangan11, Dewangan16, Burns+2019PASJ .}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, the characteristics of star clusters and their kinematics remain unstudied.
elements.sent-2411-040: The latest studies of the clusters in this region of star formation were carried out in \citet{Kirsanova08 and \citet{Camargo11 .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The more recent work of \citet{Kirsanova14 continues the study of the mechanisms of star formation in the region.}
elements.sent-2411-042: They conclude that the clusters S235~East 1, S235~East 2, and S235~Central most likely formed as a result of induced star formation caused by the expansion of the HII region.
elements.sent-2411-043: On the other hand, the S235~A-B-C cluster is quite distant from the expanding HII shell and therefore most likely has a spontaneous star formation mechanism \citep{Kirsanova08 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Such diversity in one region allows us to study how the characteristics of clusters differ depending on star formation mechanisms.

elements.sent-2411-050: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-051: In Section \ref{2 , we present an overview of the catalog data used in the work.}
elements.sent-2411-052: In Section \ref{3 , we present the NICEST absorption map in the region and discuss the selection of the filaments in our region.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Section \ref{4 is devoted to the identification of clusters and the determination of their parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The kinematics of cluster stars are discussed in Section \ref{5 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: In Section \ref{7 , we estimate the mass of some clusters.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In Section \ref{8 , we determine gas masses and star formation efficiencies in the regions of the four clusters.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Section \ref{9 includes an analysis of the possible future fate of some of the clusters under consideration.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Finally, our conclusions are summarized in Section \ref{6 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: This work uses data from the UKIDSS GPS catalog (version DR10PLUS) \citep{Lucas08 (hereinafter referred to as UKIDSS).}

elements.sent-2411-101: The NICEST method is similar to conventional methods for determining interstellar extinction in regions of low and moderate column density, but it has been specifically designed to search for small-scale structures of absorbing matter in regions of strong extinction.
elements.sent-2411-102: It is based on a comparison of the observed colors in the J, H and K bands and the stars’ absolute colors corresponding to zero absorption.
elements.sent-2411-103: We have constructed an absorption map for the G174+2.5 region with the center and radius indicated above.
elements.sent-2411-104: When constructing it, we did not consider UKIDSS catalog stars for which there was no photometric data in two bands, or for which the magnitude uncertainty was larger than $1^m$.
elements.sent-2411-105: Also due to the inaccuracy of the UKIDSS catalog for bright stars, we replaced the UKIDSS photometry with 2MASS for stars brighter than 12 mag in each band using the TOPCAT software \citep{Taylor05 .}
elements.sent-2411-106: The extinction map of the star formation region G174+2.5 was obtained with a resolution (FWHM) of 1.0--1.5 arcmin.
elements.sent-2411-107: The pixel size was set to FWHM/3.
elements.sent-2411-108: The extinction map in the star-forming region G174+2.5 is shown in Fig.\ref{FigExtMap , left panel.}
elements.sent-2411-109: It is important to note that the NICEST method is a statistical method and does not provide the ability to determine extinction for individual stars.
elements.sent-2411-110: One can readily see that the high absorption regions follow the structure of the molecular gas filaments in the region around the known clusters in Fig. \ref{FigExtMap , right panel.}

elements.sent-2411-159: Radial density profiles of all clusters in the G174+2.5 region exhibit a maximum, significantly larger than the background level.
elements.sent-2411-160: The cluster radius is determined from the radial density profile under the assumption that the cluster is spherically symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-161: The profile is built for distances from the cluster center that are several times larger than the apparent size of the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-162: Next, we consider the part of the density profile which obviously belongs to the background.
elements.sent-2411-163: The background level is drawn so that the areas of the density profile fluctuations above and below this level are the same.
elements.sent-2411-164: Then the cluster radius is defined as the abscissa of the first intersection of the background level with the radial density profile.

elements.sent-2411-181: We use the UPMASK package \citep{Krone14 to estimate the membership probability of stars in the clusters.}
elements.sent-2411-182: UPMASK does not use any model hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-183: The only assumption is the spatial homogeneity of the field star density in the considered area comparing to the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-184: Although the coordinate space plays an important role in the UPMASK method, photometric parameters (or information about proper motions and parallaxes) are also used.
elements.sent-2411-185: Initial clustering is performed using the information contained in the photometry.
elements.sent-2411-186: After this, the clustering of the selected groups in the coordinate space is checked.
elements.sent-2411-187: Thus, for a real field with some density fluctuations, in the photometric space the field stars will not be related, since they do not have a common origin.
elements.sent-2411-188: Therefore, they will not be selected as a cluster at the beginning in most iterations of the algorithm and will therefore receive a low membership probability.
elements.sent-2411-189: For our clusters we used the following parameters of the UPMASK algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-190: The minimum number of stars per cluster was chosen to be 20 in all cases.
elements.sent-2411-191: In our case, when we use smaller values in clusters 10 (S235~North-West) and 12 (S235~Central), UPMASK cannot estimate the membership probabilities of the stars.
elements.sent-2411-192: Therefore, we adopt a larger value for the parameter of stars per cluster.
elements.sent-2411-193: To obtain the probability estimate, 2,000 runs of the algorithm are performed for stars with known photometry in all three UKIDSS bands (J, H, K).
elements.sent-2411-194: We use the equatorial coordinates of stars, magnitudes in the J, H and K bands, color indices J-H, J-K, and H-K and the errors in these magnitudes as the input data for UPMASK.
elements.sent-2411-195: This algorithm is used only for clusters with well-defined clustering of stars on the maps, namely 10 (S235~North-West), 11 (S235~A-B-C), 12 (S235~Central) and 14 (S235~East1+East2).

elements.sent-2411-198: For clusters 10 (S235~North-West) and 12 (S235~Central) we had to use circles with a radius of twice the cluster radius, since the smaller regions did not contain enough stars for UPMASK to work.

elements.sent-2411-264: To study the clusters' internal kinematics, we consider the radial and tangential velocity components of the cluster members.
elements.sent-2411-265: These velocity components are plotted relative to the center of the cluster in the tangential plane.
elements.sent-2411-266: The dependence of these velocity components on the position of stars in the cluster can help to track possible rotation of the cluster, its compression or expansion, possible irregularities of motion within the cluster, and differences in the velocity distribution of YSOs and other stars in the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-267: Here, we mean the position of the star in the cluster as the distance from the center of the cluster and the position angle of the star relative to the $RA$ axis.
elements.sent-2411-268: In the case of the translational motion of the cluster stars in the absence of any internal motions of the stars, we should see a clear sinusoidal sequence on the plots `velocity component~-- positional angle'.
elements.sent-2411-269: This sinusoidal sequence should have the same amplitudes of maximum and minimum and the distance of $\pi$ radians between the points of intersection of the sequence with the abscissa axis.
elements.sent-2411-270: When we add some bulk rotation over the translational motion, the sinusoidal sequence on the plot `tangential component~-- positional angle' will show a displacement up or down along the ordinate axis (the tangential velocity component).
elements.sent-2411-271: When we include expansion or contraction of the cluster kinematics, we will see a similar displacement in the `radial component~-- positional angle' plot.
elements.sent-2411-272: If the apex of the cluster stars motion is located close to the cluster (projected onto the celestial sphere), or the cluster has some other internal motion, the humps of the sinusoidal sequence becomes asymmetrical.
elements.sent-2411-273: When the cluster stars in the outer parts of the cluster have a larger velocity dispersion comparing to the cluster core, the plots `velocity components~-- distance' will show an increase in the scatter of values with distance.

elements.sent-2411-287: Since we know the probabilities of membership of stars in clusters 10 (S235~North-West), 11 (S235~A-B-C), 12 (S235~Central), and 14 (S235~East1+East2), we can attempt to estimate their masses.
elements.sent-2411-288: In order to achieve this, we use two methods: the first is the comparison with the evolutionary tracks of stars, and the second is a dynamic mass estimate.
elements.sent-2411-289: We use evolutionary tracks of the Padova suite of models \citep {Bressan12 to determine the mass of clusters from the color-magnitude diagrams.}
elements.sent-2411-290: We use evolutionary tracks with masses from 0.1 to 8 $M_{\odot}$ with step of 0.1 $M_{\odot}$ for masses less than 1.0 $M_{\odot}$ and step of 0.5 for masses greater than 1.0 $M_{\odot}$.

elements.sent-2411-292: For stars located to the right of the track for the lowest-mass star, we assume the masses of all stars equal to 0.1 $M_{\odot}$.
elements.sent-2411-293: Calculations are carried out for tracks built in the `H-K versus K' color magnitude diagram.

elements.sent-2411-414: Recently, \citet{Danilov24 considered the problem of the dynamics of an embedded cluster inside the parental gas-dust cloud within the framework of the gross dynamic approach.}
elements.sent-2411-415: We use his results to evaluate the total energy of the clusters with the aim to estimate their possible future.
elements.sent-2411-416: If we determine the sign of the total energy, we can infer whether the system is gravitationally bound or not.
elements.sent-2411-417: The derivation of the total energy equations is based on the method of dynamically isolated k groups (clusters) of stars, developed in \citet{Danilov84 .}
elements.sent-2411-418: It allows us to describe the dynamics of spherically symmetric clusters of stars that are nonstationary in a regular field.
elements.sent-2411-419: In such systems, changes in the total energies of groups of stars are caused by the interactions of these groups with each other and their interactions with the variable force field of the system.
elements.sent-2411-420: This method is also applicable to describe cluster-gas systems.
elements.sent-2411-421: The total energy is calculated on the assumption that the system consists of two components~-- stars (1) and gas (2).
elements.sent-2411-422: It is assumed that the radius of the stellar component is less than or equal to the radius of the gas component.
elements.sent-2411-423: In the method used, both subsystems are considered as homogeneous gravitating spheres with coinciding centers of mass.
elements.sent-2411-424: For each of these subsystems we need to know the mass, radius and velocity dispersion.
elements.sent-2411-425: To estimate the kinetic energy of the stellar and gaseous subsystems, we used the equation \begin{equation}\label{Eq9.1} T_i=\frac{M_i\sigma_i^2}{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-426: Here $M_i$ is the mass of the cluster or gas, $\sigma_i^2$ is the velocity dispersion of the cluster stars or the dispersion of non-thermal gas motion.
elements.sent-2411-427: The potential energy of the system consists of the potential energy of each of the subsystems (first term in every equation) and the energy of their interaction (second term): \begin{equation}\label{Eq9.2} \Omega_1=-\frac{3GM_1^2}{5R_1}-\frac{3GM_1M_2}{2R_2}\left(1-\frac{R_1^2}{5R_2^2}\right),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{Eq9.3} \Omega_2=-\frac{3GM_2^2}{5R_2}-\frac{3GM_1M_2}{2R_2}\left(1-\frac{R_1^2}{5R_2^2}\right),\end{equation} where $M_i$ are masses, $R_i$ are radii of subsystems, $G$ is gravitational constant.
elements.sent-2411-428: Thus, the total energy of the system results: \begin{equation}\label{Eq9.4} E=E_1+E_2- \Omega_{12}=T_1+\Omega_1+T_2+\Omega_2 - \Omega_{12},\end{equation} where $\Omega_{12}$ is the potential energy of interaction of the stellar subsystem with the gas subsystem \begin{equation}\label{Eq9.5} \Omega_{12}=-\frac{3GM_1M_2}{2R_2}\left(1-\frac{R_1^2}{5R_2^2}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-429: It is worth highlighting that the calculations in \citet{Danilov24 were carried out for the case when all subsystems are spherically symmetric and their centers coincide.}

elements.sent-2411-455: We found 14 clusters on the surface density map, 6 of which are previously unknown embedded clusters.
elements.sent-2411-456: All detected clusters are located in the molecular gas filaments.
elements.sent-2411-457: In the surface density map of the S235~A-B-C cluster region, we also found a number of local density maxima.
elements.sent-2411-458: We consider these density maxima to be sub-clusters of S235~A-B-C rather than separate clusters.
elements.sent-2411-459: For all clusters, we estimated the radii and field density levels in the cluster areas using the KDE method.
elements.sent-2411-460: Also, we have counted the cluster star numbers and for 4 clusters estimated the membership probabilities of stars using the UPMASK package.
elements.sent-2411-461: The individual reddening of stars in the region was determined using the NICEST method and Q-method at the base of the UKIDSS photometry.
elements.sent-2411-462: Using NICEST data, we built the map of the absorption distribution in the region.
elements.sent-2411-463: The areas of increased absorption coincide with the filaments of molecular gas identified by the GetFilaments algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-464: The estimates of distance to clusters 10 (S235~North-West), 11 (S235~A-B-C), 12 (S235~Central) and 14 (S235~East1+East2) are in the range from 1.7 to 2.2 kpc.
elements.sent-2411-465: It indicates that these clusters belong to the studied star formation region.
elements.sent-2411-466: We use the proper motion data from Gaia DR3 to estimate the average velocities of stars in clusters.
elements.sent-2411-467: Also we study the internal kinematics of stars in some clusters: clusters 10 (S235~North-West) and 11 (S235~A-B-C appear to move as a single units (with respect to their centers) without noticeable internal motions of stars, while cluster 14 (S235~East1+East2) may have irregularities of stellar movement.
elements.sent-2411-468: The masses of four clusters were determined in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-469: The dynamic method gives greatly overestimated values, which is due to the lack of consideration of the gas component and large errors in determining proper motions.
elements.sent-2411-470: On the contrary, masses determined photometrically may be underestimated.
elements.sent-2411-471: This is possible because stars with large magnitudes are not visible due to limitations in the completeness of the catalog and high extinction in the region.
elements.sent-2411-472: The total mass of the clusters was obtained using Kroupa's initial mass function.
elements.sent-2411-473: Additionally, based on the emission data of the lines of the ${ ^{12}$CO(1-0) and ${ ^{13}$CO(1-0), the gas masses in the areas of clusters were determined.}}
elements.sent-2411-474: We estimated star formation efficiency at the base of the obtained masses of gas and cluster stars.
elements.sent-2411-475: SFE ranges from 0.04 to 0.17, that corresponds to the values of star formation efficiency in the Milky Way known from the literature.
elements.sent-2411-476: The lowest estimates are observed in regions 12 (S235~Central) and 14 (S235~East1+East2) with induced star formation.
elements.sent-2411-477: The SFE estimate in the 10 (S235~North-West) cluster (also with induced star formation) may be exaggerated due to an underrating of the gas mass.
elements.sent-2411-478: Thus, in the region under consideration, induced star formation is characterized by lower SFE values, while spontaneous star formation (cluster 11 (S235~A-B-C)) is characterized by slightly higher values.
elements.sent-2411-479: However, the differences in the SFE estimates between clusters are not significant enough to confidently assert the connection between SFE and the cluster formation process.
elements.sent-2411-480: The total energies of four clusters were estimated considering both stellar and gas components.
elements.sent-2411-481: The gravitational boundness of the systems under consideration depends on the region for which the gas mass is estimated.
elements.sent-2411-482: Thus, when we take into account the mass of the entire cloud, all four cluster-gas systems appear to be gravitationally bound.
elements.sent-2411-483: When we take into account the gas only in the cluster regions, only clusters 12 (S235~Central) and 14 (S235~East1+East2) are probably gravitationally bound.
elements.sent-2411-484: It should be noted that both star counts and the analysis of the dynamical state of the clusters in our paper are based on an assumption of a spherical symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-485: However, the real structure and formation path are expected to differ from one cluster to another.
elements.sent-2411-486: Star formation in the clusters S235~North-West (No. 10 in Table 1), S235~Central (No. 12 in Table 1), S235~East1,East2 (No. 14 in Table 1) was most probably induced by an expanding HII region Sh2-235 \citep{Kirsanova08,Kirsanova14 .}
elements.sent-2411-487: Consequently, these clusters have a flattened form.
elements.sent-2411-488: Star formation in the cluster S235~A-B-C (No. 11 in Table 1) was on the other hand spontaneous.
elements.sent-2411-489: Nevertheless, this cluster has a non-uniform structure and consists of some sub-clusters (Fig. 4d).
elements.sent-2411-490: Besides, the formation of some clusters in the envelope complicates the determination of the gas mass.
elements.sent-2411-491: A remarkable part of the gas there is ionized and is not visible in the molecular lines.
elements.sent-2411-492: Thus, our investigation can be considered as an attempt to use simple models for a complex star formation region.
elements.sent-2411-493: It is interesting that star formation region G174+2.5 seems to be a part of a larger structure containing HII regions, Supernova remnants, and O-B stars.
elements.sent-2411-494: This complex has a dumbbell structure with the approximate center at (173,0) and the larger axis perpendicular to the Galactic plane (see Fig.5 in \citet{Kang+2012 ).}
elements.sent-2411-495: This possibly indicates an even more tangled star formation history.
elements.sent-2411-496: However, to get a more detailed picture of the cluster kinematics and dynamics in this fascinating region, we need high resolution IR photometry and astrometry.

elements.section-2411-010: Determination of the interstellar absorption using the Q-method
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=11cm]{Q.pdf} \caption{The zero-reddening sequence. The Pleiades and Praesepe stars are shown by the gray dots, the non-reddened sequence described by the Eq.(\ref{ApEq_left}) and Eq.(\ref{ApEq_right}) is shown by solid black lines. Blue lines are the boundaries of the non-reddened sequence. The red dotted lines show the coordinates of the intersection point of the segments (see text for explanations).} \label{FigQ}
elements.sent-2411-504: To estimate the individual absorption for stars, we used the Q-method first proposed by \citet{Johnson53 .}
elements.sent-2411-505: The essence of the method consists in comparing the position of the star relative to the non-reddened sequence on a Q-parameter versus color index diagram.
elements.sent-2411-506: The parameter Q is extinction-free \citep{Johnson53 , and is constructed from a combination of the color indices and a ratio of the color excesses.}
elements.sent-2411-507: This ratio can be obtained from the extinction law.
elements.sent-2411-508: For UKIDSS photometry we use the following expression for the Q parameter: \begin{equation}\label{ApEq1} Q=(J-H)-k_R(H-K),\end{equation} where $(J-H)$ and $(H-K)$ are color indices, $k_R=E(J-H)/E(H-K)=1.423$ \citep{Cardelli89,O'Donnell94 , $E(J-H)$ and $E(H-K)$ are color excesses for the corresponding color indices.}
elements.sent-2411-509: We plot the zero-reddening sequence using the stars of the Pleiades and Praesepe star clusters (from \citet {Lodieu19 ).}
elements.sent-2411-510: Then, we approximate this sequence by the least squares on the plane (the parameter Q, the color index H-K) by two segments of a parabola (Fig.\ref{FigQ ).}
elements.sent-2411-511: Since the magnitudes of the stars in the Pleiades and Praesepe clusters are given in the 2MASS photometric system, they were transformed into the UKIDSS photometric standard using equations (6)–(8) of \citet{Hodgkin09 .}
elements.sent-2411-512: For fitting, we use the curve\_fit function of the Python SciPy library.
elements.sent-2411-513: We obtained the following equations for the left and right segments, respectively: \begin{equation}\label{ApEq_left} (H-K)=0.1573\cdot Q^2+0.2338\cdot Q+0.0157 ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{ApEq_right} (H-K)=0.0337\cdot Q^2-0.4941\cdot Q+0.3561 .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-514: We calculate the color excess of a star as follows.
elements.sent-2411-515: For a star, the parameter Q is calculated using the equation \ref{ApEq1 .}
elements.sent-2411-516: This Q value is then substituted into the equation of left (Eq. \ref{ApEq_left ) or right (Eq. \ref{ApEq_right ) segment to obtain the dereddened color index.}}
elements.sent-2411-517: The segment selection method is described below.
elements.sent-2411-518: Color excess is calculated as the difference between the original color index and the calculated absolute color index.
elements.sent-2411-519: We convert the color excess obtained by the Q-method to the absorption in the selected band using the coefficients obtained by \citet{Cardelli89 and \citet{O'Donnell94 from the extinction curve: $A_J/A_V=0.28170$, $A_H/A_V=0.18251$ and $A_K/A_V=0.11281$, so $A_K\approx1.6185E(H-K)$ and $A_K\approx1.1373E(J-H)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-520: These coefficients are extracted from the isochrone tables \citep{Bressan12 .}
elements.sent-2411-521: A limitation of the use of the Q-method is the range of Q values for which the zero-reddening sequence is determined, in this work it is $Q\in[-0.10; 0.44]$.
elements.sent-2411-522: In the plots `parameter Q~--~color index' for clusters of the G174+2.5 region, we see both stars which lie close to the zero-reddening sequence, and reddened stars located to the right of the absolute sequences.
elements.sent-2411-523: We consider as unextincted those stars whose distance to the right segment (Eq. \ref{ApEq_right ) of the zero-reddening sequence along the H-K color index axis is less than 0.022.}
elements.sent-2411-524: This value is the half-width of the right zero-reddening sequence.
elements.sent-2411-525: We determine the width of each segment of the zero-reddening sequence individually by shifting the corresponding segment rightward and leftward along the color index axis.
elements.sent-2411-526: The shift to the left or to the right was carried out until 10\% of the Pleiades and Praesepe stars belonging to this segment remained to the left or right of it, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-527: Thus, between the boundaries of one segment there are 80\% of the stars belonging to this segment.
elements.sent-2411-528: The distance between the shifted sequences of the segment was taken as the width of this segment.
elements.sent-2411-529: The width of the left segment along the color index axis turns out to be 0.098, while that of the right segment is 0.044.
elements.sent-2411-530: The boundaries of the un-reddened sequence obtained in this way are indicated in Fig. \ref{FigQ by blue lines.}
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=10cm]{LumFunc.pdf} \caption{Combined luminosity functions of cluster 11 (S235~A-B-C) and the Pleiades. The luminosity function of the Pleiades stars belonging to the left segment is shown in orange, and the luminosity function of the right segment is shown in blue. The luminosity function of cluster 11 (S235~A-B-C) is shown in red. The black vertical line is the limiting value.} \label{FigLumFunc}
elements.sent-2411-531: When using the Q-method, the main problem is choosing which segment of zero-reddening sequence a star belongs to, without knowing the spectral type of that star.
elements.sent-2411-532: We tried to get around this issue in the following way.
elements.sent-2411-533: In the Pleiades and Praesepe luminosity functions constructed using absolute magnitudes, the stars of the left and right segment occupy different magnitude ranges.
elements.sent-2411-534: The stars on the left segment are brighter, while the stars on the right segment are dimmer.
elements.sent-2411-535: These ranges overlap only in a small range of values (see Fig. \ref{FigLumFunc ).}
elements.sent-2411-536: A similar separation is observed for all adopted bands.
elements.sent-2411-537: We define a magnitude limit at which the number of stars in the left segment still significantly exceeds the number of stars in the right segment.
elements.sent-2411-538: The limit value is the same in both clusters when using the absolute magnitudes.
elements.sent-2411-539: When constructing the luminosity function from apparent magnitudes, the initial limit value is shifted by the distance modulus of the corresponding cluster.
elements.sent-2411-540: In this case, the sizes of the ranges of values in which the luminosity functions of the stars of the left and right segments are located, and the areas of their intersection, do not change.
elements.sent-2411-541: Their relative position does not change either.
elements.sent-2411-542: Therefore, we assume that the same separation of stars in the left and right segments exists for the clusters under study.
elements.sent-2411-543: Then, by combining the luminosity function of the Pleiades and Praesepe with the luminosity function of the cluster, it is possible to approximately determine the limit value of the magnitude of our clusters.
elements.sent-2411-544: For all stars in the cluster whose magnitude is less than this limiting value, the color excess is determined by the left segment (Eq. \ref{ApEq_left ) of the absolute sequence, for the rest~-- by the right (Eq. \ref{ApEq_right ).}}
elements.sent-2411-545: The combination is carried out as follows.
elements.sent-2411-546: First, we add the average extinction for the cluster under study, determined by the NICEST method, to the absolute magnitudes of the Pleiades and Praesepe stars.
elements.sent-2411-547: Then, we shift these luminosity functions to the right until their left edge coincides with the left edge of the cluster's luminosity function.
elements.sent-2411-548: From these shifted luminosity functions of the Pleiades and Praesepe, the limiting value for the cluster is determined.
elements.sent-2411-549: An example of combining the luminosity function of cluster 11 (S235~A-B-C) and the Pleiades luminosity function is shown in Fig. \ref{FigLumFunc .}
elements.sent-2411-550: We also note that when using limiting values defined for the same cluster but for different bands, the number of stars assigned to the left segment might vary.
elements.sent-2411-551: When using this method of selecting the segment from which the absorption is determined, a discontinuity may occur in the photometric sequences on the CMD of clusters.
elements.sent-2411-552: This is due to the fact that unreddened stars do not lie strictly on the absolute sequence, but occupy a region around it (limited by blue lines in Fig. \ref{FigQ ).}
elements.sent-2411-553: If there are no or few stars in the cluster with a Q parameter near 0.44, then, after correction for extinction, there will also be few stars on the CMD in the region with H-K color indices of about 0.15.
elements.sent-2411-554: These values are the coordinates of the intersection point of the left and right segments of the absolute sequence (marked by the red dotted line in Fig. \ref{FigQ ).. \bibliographystyle{aasjournal.}}
